correctedCalcium.title: Corrected Calcium Calculator
correctedCalcium.description: Calculate albumin-corrected serum calcium with the Payne formula in conventional or SI units, with clinical classification.
correctedCalcium.albuminHint: Normal albumin: 4.0 g/dL or 40 g/L. Without albumin no correction is possible.
correctedCalcium.warningAlbuminMissing: Albumin missing. The albumin value is required to compute the correction.
correctedCalcium.warningPlausibility: Input value outside the physiologic range. Please check your input.

hint.hypocalcemia: Hypocalcemia after albumin correction. Causes: vitamin D deficiency, hypoparathyroidism, CKD, acute pancreatitis, severe sepsis. Verify with clinical context and consider ionized calcium.
hint.normal: Corrected calcium within the normal range (8.5–10.2 mg/dL or 2.12–2.55 mmol/L).
hint.hypercalcemia: Hypercalcemia after correction. Most common causes: primary hyperparathyroidism and malignancy. Workup: PTH, vitamin D, phosphate, kidney function.

referenceRanges.label: Hypercalcemia
referenceRanges.hint: DDx: HPT, malignancy, vitamin D toxicity

correctedCalcium.disclaimer: The Payne correction is unreliable in severe acid-base disturbances. Ionized calcium is the gold standard. This calculator does not replace clinical diagnosis.
correctedCalcium.blogLink: Read more in the corrected calcium article

faq.a: About 40% of serum calcium is bound to albumin. In hypoalbuminemia the measured total calcium appears falsely low even though the physiologically active ionized calcium may be normal. The Payne formula compensates for this effect.
Corrected Ca [mg/dL] = Total Ca + 0.8 × (4.0 − Albumin [g/dL]). In SI units: Corrected Ca [mmol/L] = Total Ca + 0.02 × (40 − Albumin [g/L]).
In severe acid-base disturbances, multiple myeloma (paraprotein-related pseudohypercalcemia), and in critically ill patients. In those cases, ionized calcium should be measured directly.

faq.q: Which normal albumin value is used?
faq.a: The formula uses 4.0 g/dL (or 40 g/L) as the reference. At exactly this albumin value, no correction occurs — total and corrected calcium are identical.

faq.q: Does this calculator replace medical diagnosis?
faq.a: No. Albumin-corrected calcium is an interpretation aid and must be considered together with clinical context (symptoms, PTH, vitamin D, kidney function).
